A food market
This dish is cooked at home.
The whey is simmered for hours.
It is eaten on bread every morning.
The recipe has been passed down for generations.
Welcome to the food market! This little stall is where the best brown cheese is made. It is our great specialty here, have you tried it?
Not yet. It looks fascinating. How is it actually made?
The whey is simmered very slowly for hours, until the sugar turns brown and sweet. Then it is pressed into blocks. The milk is all locally sourced, from farms just up the valley.
A friend once told me it was sweet and savory at the same time. Is it eaten on its own?
Usually it is served on thick bread, for breakfast. For a first-timer it is an acquired taste, but the whole country is renowned for it. Here, try a slice.
Mmm. Strange and wonderful. Back home, we have a cheese that is aged in caves, I will have to bring you some.
It is made from just a few simple ingredients.
It is served with rice and greens.
The ingredients are locally sourced.
It is an acquired taste, but I love it.
My region is renowned for it.

Hi Marcelo, it is Linnea. I promised I would tell you how my grandmother's fish soup is made, so here it is, before I forget. First, the fish heads and bones are simmered very slowly for a whole afternoon, until the broth turns rich and golden. Nothing is wasted, that is the whole point. Then the vegetables are added, always whatever is in season, and a little cream at the end. It is served with dark bread and a lot of talking. It is not a fancy dish, it is not a delicacy, but it is loved by everyone in my family, and the recipe has never been written down. It is only passed down, from one cook to the next, by watching. That is what I love about it. Come and try it when you are here. Bye for now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, why food is the fastest way into a culture. Here is the thing. You can read every guidebook ever written, but a place is not really understood until it is tasted. Think about it. When a dish is put in front of you, a whole history comes with it. What is grown in that soil. What has been traded there for centuries. What is celebrated, and when. A staple is never just food. It is memory, pressed onto a plate. And the beauty is that you do not need perfect grammar to reach it. You just need to be curious. Ask how something is made. Ask what it is served with, and why. And almost always, the person will slow down, and smile, and tell you far more than a recipe. Because when you ask about the food, you are really asking about them.
